feed.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge

entry.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge
entry.summary:
The discussion effectively highlights advancements in cryptographic proof times through optimized GPU usage, specifically addressing the significant reduction in prover time from 20 seconds to an impressive 2 seconds. This enhancement primarily results from the strategic implementation of pre-seeding SHA512 midstates, which potentially cuts down the number of necessary compression calls from four to two. Such an optimization could further reduce the prover time to just over one second while also decreasing verifier time, as it is influenced by circuit complexity.
Moreover, there are strong arguments presented regarding the security implications of this approach. Firstly, even with access to correct midstates, a fraudulent prover still faces the substantial challenge of uncovering the parent secret key (`sk`), which retains a difficulty level comparable to direct hashing without a chaincode. The process adjustment affects only the midstate and the input length suffix for SHA512, not simplifying the fundamental task of resolving for `sk`.
Secondly, the feasibility of adversaries successfully finding the correct midstates, which could hypothetically act as an oracle for proving partial 2-cycles in SHA512, is analyzed. The conditions required for a successful fraudulent proof involve not only acquiring the right midstates but also ensuring these midstates enable a specific 2-stage partial hash cycle—a scenario deemed highly improbable due to its complexity. This adds an additional layer of security against potential exploitation by reducing the likelihood of such occurrences. Thus, the proposed method not only enhances efficiency but also maintains robust security measures.
